efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: With **sand, shore, tide, wave, dune**, don't lock onto the first connection you see. Try different groupings and pick the one where **all 3 clearly belong** and the **2 leftovers also connect**.
lessonLearningGoal: How to avoid being tricked by words that LOOK like they belong together but actually don't — the 11+ loves this trick!
Why your first instinct can sometimes lead you astray, and how to double-check before committing

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **bucket, hammer, jug, vase, spanner**. Ask yourself: **"What is each thing used for?"** Things with the same purpose belong together, even if they look completely different.
lessonLearningGoal: How to group words by what things DO rather than what they ARE — this is a really clever way to think!
Why asking 'what is this used for?' can reveal hidden connections that aren't obvious at first

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **apple, carrot, banana, grape, potato**. Here's the clever bit: don't hunt for the two odd ones. Instead, find the **three** words that connect — once you spot the group of 3, the odd pair re
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot obvious category groups like animals, fruits, colours, and sports — you'll get a feel for this really quickly!
How to find the 3-and-2 split where three words share a group and the other two are the odd ones out

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **apple, carrot, banana, grape, potato**. Here's the clever bit: don't hunt for the two odd ones. Instead, find the **three** words that connect — once you spot the group of 3, the odd pair re
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot obvious category groups like animals, fruits, colours, and sports — you'll get a feel for this really quickly!
How to find the 3-and-2 split where three words share a group and the other two are the odd ones out

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **serene, furious, tranquil, placid, irate** — these are abstract (idea) words. Ask yourself: **what does each word describe?** Group by meaning, not by how they look or sound.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot less obvious groups like emotions, qualities, and weather types — these are the ones that trip people up!
Why abstract categories need a bit more digging than simple ones (and why that makes them more satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **iron, cotton, steel, bronze, silk**. Ask: **"What is each thing made of?"** or **"What type of material is this?"** Three will share a material type, two won't.
lessonLearningGoal: How to group words by what things are made of: metals, fabrics, woods
Why material-based categories are a common 11+ trick

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **bucket, hammer, jug, vase, spanner**. Ask yourself: **"What is each thing used for?"** Things with the same purpose belong together, even if they look completely different.
lessonLearningGoal: How to group words by what things DO rather than what they ARE — this is a really clever way to think!
Why asking 'what is this used for?' can reveal hidden connections that aren't obvious at first

lessonLearningGoal: How to group words by what things DO rather than what they ARE — this is a really clever way to think!
Why asking 'what is this used for?' can reveal hidden connections that aren't obvious at first

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **serene, furious, tranquil, placid, irate** — these are abstract (idea) words. Ask yourself: **what does each word describe?** Group by meaning, not by how they look or sound.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot less obvious groups like emotions, qualities, and weather types — these are the ones that trip people up!
Why abstract categories need a bit more digging than simple ones (and why that makes them more satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **serene, furious, tranquil, placid, irate** — these are abstract (idea) words. Ask yourself: **what does each word describe?** Group by meaning, not by how they look or sound.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot less obvious groups like emotions, qualities, and weather types — these are the ones that trip people up!
Why abstract categories need a bit more digging than simple ones (and why that makes them more satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **iron, cotton, steel, bronze, silk**. Ask: **"What is each thing made of?"** or **"What type of material is this?"** Three will share a material type, two won't.
lessonLearningGoal: How to group words by what things are made of: metals, fabrics, woods
Why material-based categories are a common 11+ trick

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: With **sand, shore, tide, wave, dune**, don't lock onto the first connection you see. Try different groupings and pick the one where **all 3 clearly belong** and the **2 leftovers also connect**.
lessonLearningGoal: How to avoid being tricked by words that LOOK like they belong together but actually don't — the 11+ loves this trick!
Why your first instinct can sometimes lead you astray, and how to double-check before committing

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **simile, adjective, metaphor, personification, adverb**. You don't need to know every word — if you can spot **2 or 3 words** from the same subject area, you can work out the grouping.
lessonLearningGoal: How to handle odd-two-out when the words look scary: literary, scientific, and musical terms
Why knowing a few key vocabulary sets gives you a massive advantage (you'll recognise more than you think!)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **apple, carrot, banana, grape, potato**. Here's the clever bit: don't hunt for the two odd ones. Instead, find the **three** words that connect — once you spot the group of 3, the odd pair re
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot obvious category groups like animals, fruits, colours, and sports — you'll get a feel for this really quickly!
How to find the 3-and-2 split where three words share a group and the other two are the odd ones out

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **bucket, hammer, jug, vase, spanner**. Ask yourself: **"What is each thing used for?"** Things with the same purpose belong together, even if they look completely different.
lessonLearningGoal: How to group words by what things DO rather than what they ARE — this is a really clever way to think!
Why asking 'what is this used for?' can reveal hidden connections that aren't obvious at first

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **serene, furious, tranquil, placid, irate** — these are abstract (idea) words. Ask yourself: **what does each word describe?** Group by meaning, not by how they look or sound.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot less obvious groups like emotions, qualities, and weather types — these are the ones that trip people up!
Why abstract categories need a bit more digging than simple ones (and why that makes them more satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **serene, furious, tranquil, placid, irate** — these are abstract (idea) words. Ask yourself: **what does each word describe?** Group by meaning, not by how they look or sound.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot less obvious groups like emotions, qualities, and weather types — these are the ones that trip people up!
Why abstract categories need a bit more digging than simple ones (and why that makes them more satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **iron, cotton, steel, bronze, silk**. Ask: **"What is each thing made of?"** or **"What type of material is this?"** Three will share a material type, two won't.
lessonLearningGoal: How to group words by what things are made of: metals, fabrics, woods
Why material-based categories are a common 11+ trick

efficacyPairs.questionText: Find the two words that are the odd ones out.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **simile, adjective, metaphor, personification, adverb**. You don't need to know every word — if you can spot **2 or 3 words** from the same subject area, you can work out the grouping.
lessonLearningGoal: How to handle odd-two-out when the words look scary: literary, scientific, and musical terms
Why knowing a few key vocabulary sets gives you a massive advantage (you'll recognise more than you think!)

lessonLearningGoal: How to handle odd-two-out when the words look scary: literary, scientific, and musical terms
Why knowing a few key vocabulary sets gives you a massive advantage (you'll recognise more than you think!)
